questions.stem: A habitat is best defined as:

options.text: The place in which an organism, population, species or community lives
The role of a species in an ecosystem
The total number of species on Earth

questions.explanation: A habitat is the place where a community, species, population or organism lives.

questions.stem: A description of a species' habitat can include:

options.text: Geographical and physical location and ecosystem type

questions.explanation: Habitat descriptions can include geographical and physical location and the type of ecosystem.

questions.scenario: A grass species grows on shifting, dry sand dunes.
questions.stem: Its features that suit it to this environment are examples of:
questions.explanation: Features suiting an organism to non-living conditions, like a dune grass, are adaptations to the abiotic environment.

questions.scenario: A tree species grows in salty, waterlogged mangrove swamps.
questions.stem: Its specialized features are adaptations to:

options.text: The abiotic conditions of its habitat

questions.explanation: Mangrove trees show adaptations to abiotic conditions such as salinity and waterlogging.

questions.stem: An abiotic variable is a factor that is:

options.text: Non-living, such as temperature or pH

questions.explanation: Abiotic variables are non-living factors such as temperature, light and pH that can affect species distribution.

questions.stem: The adaptations of a species give it a range of:
questions.explanation: A species' adaptations give it a range of tolerance for abiotic variables, affecting where it can live.

questions.scenario: A student samples plants along a line across a habitat to relate distribution to soil pH.
questions.stem: This sampling method is called a:
questions.explanation: A transect is a line along which data are collected to correlate species distribution with an abiotic variable.

questions.scenario: A semi-natural habitat is used for fieldwork.
questions.stem: A semi-natural habitat is one that:

options.text: Has been influenced by humans but is dominated by wild species

questions.explanation: Semi-natural habitats have been influenced by humans but are dominated by wild rather than cultivated species.

questions.stem: Which device could measure an abiotic variable during fieldwork?
questions.explanation: Sensors can measure abiotic variables such as temperature, light intensity and soil pH.

questions.scenario: Coral reefs are used as an example of a marine ecosystem.
questions.stem: Which factor is required for coral reef formation?
questions.explanation: Coral reef formation requires suitable water depth, pH, salinity, clarity and temperature — generally warm, clear, shallow water.

questions.stem: Which of these is a factor affecting coral reef formation?

options.text: The number of genes in coral

questions.explanation: Factors for coral reef formation include water depth, pH, salinity, clarity and temperature.

questions.stem: Which two climatic variables largely determine which terrestrial biome develops?
questions.explanation: For a given temperature and rainfall pattern, one natural ecosystem type is likely to develop.

questions.stem: Abiotic factors are described as the determinants of:
questions.explanation: Abiotic factors, chiefly temperature and rainfall, determine which terrestrial biome develops in a region.

questions.stem: A biome is a group of ecosystems with similar communities due to:

options.text: Similar abiotic conditions and convergent evolution

questions.explanation: Biomes are groups of ecosystems with similar communities arising from similar abiotic conditions and convergent evolution.

questions.stem: Which of these is one of the biomes students should recognise by its climate?
questions.explanation: Recognised terrestrial biomes include tropical forest, temperate forest, taiga, grassland, tundra and hot desert.

questions.scenario: A desert plant has thick, waxy leaves and stores water in its stem.
questions.stem: These are adaptations to life in a:
questions.explanation: Water-conserving features such as waxy leaves and water storage are adaptations to hot deserts.

questions.scenario: A rainforest plant grows tall quickly to reach light through dense canopy.
questions.stem: This is an adaptation to life in:
questions.explanation: Rapid growth toward light is an adaptation to the tropical rainforest, where competition for light is intense.

questions.stem: Which correctly matches the term 'habitat'?

options.text: What an organism does in its ecosystem

questions.explanation: Habitat is where an organism lives; the role it plays is its ecological niche.

questions.scenario: A plant can only survive within a certain range of soil pH.
questions.stem: Outside this range it cannot thrive because it has exceeded its:
questions.explanation: Each species has a range of tolerance for an abiotic variable; beyond it, the species cannot survive well.

questions.scenario: A transect shows a plant becomes more common as soil moisture increases.
questions.stem: This suggests the plant's distribution is:
questions.explanation: Transect data can reveal correlations between species distribution and abiotic variables such as soil moisture.

questions.stem: Adaptations of organisms to the abiotic environment mean their features:

options.text: Suit them to the non-living conditions of their habitat
Have no relationship to their surroundings
Are the same in every habitat

questions.explanation: Adaptations to the abiotic environment are features that suit organisms to non-living conditions like temperature and salinity.

questions.stem: Which biome is characterised by cold conditions and coniferous forest?
questions.explanation: The taiga (boreal forest) is a cold biome dominated by coniferous trees.

questions.scenario: Two rainforests on different continents contain unrelated species that look and function similarly.
questions.stem: This similarity between biomes is largely due to:

options.text: Similar abiotic conditions driving convergent evolution
Recent common ancestry of all the species

questions.explanation: Similar communities in the same biome on different continents arise from similar abiotic conditions and convergent evolution.

questions.scenario: A graph plots biome distribution against temperature and rainfall.
questions.stem: What does such a graph show?

options.text: Which biome develops for a given temperature and rainfall
The DNA sequence of each species

questions.explanation: Such graphs show how biomes are distributed according to the two climatic variables of temperature and rainfall.

questions.scenario: A desert animal is active at night and rests in burrows by day.
questions.stem: This behaviour is an adaptation to:

options.text: Avoiding extreme daytime heat and water loss

questions.explanation: Being nocturnal and sheltering by day are adaptations that help desert animals avoid heat and reduce water loss.

questions.stem: Which of these could be part of a habitat description?

options.text: The type of ecosystem the organism lives in
The number of ribosomes per cell

questions.explanation: A habitat description can include geographical and physical location and the type of ecosystem.

questions.stem: Which is an example of an abiotic variable affecting animals?
questions.explanation: Abiotic variables are non-living factors such as temperature; predators, mates and prey are biotic factors.

questions.scenario: A coral reef is found in shallow, sunlit water.
questions.stem: Why is water clarity important for reefs?

options.text: It allows light to reach the reef for photosynthesis
It cools the water to freezing

questions.explanation: Clear water lets light reach the reef, supporting the photosynthetic algae associated with corals.

questions.stem: Which biome is characterised by very cold conditions and low-growing vegetation with no trees?
questions.explanation: The tundra is a very cold biome with low-growing vegetation and no trees.

questions.stem: Adaptations of species to hot deserts often focus on:
questions.explanation: Desert species commonly have adaptations for conserving water given the dry, hot conditions.

questions.scenario: A region has high temperature and low rainfall.
questions.stem: Which biome is most likely to develop there?
questions.explanation: High temperature with low rainfall favours the development of a hot desert biome.

questions.scenario: Mangrove trees have specialized roots that cope with waterlogged, salty soil.
questions.stem: These roots are an example of adaptation to:
questions.explanation: Specialized mangrove roots are adaptations to abiotic conditions such as salinity and waterlogging.

questions.scenario: A student collects transect data from a semi-natural habitat.
questions.stem: Why is this preferable to inventing the data?

options.text: First-hand data supports valid conclusions about distribution
Data is not needed at all

questions.explanation: Students collect the data themselves so they can validly correlate species distribution with abiotic variables.

questions.stem: Grassland and temperate forest are examples of:
questions.explanation: Grassland and temperate forest are among the terrestrial biomes recognised by their climate conditions.

questions.stem: Why does coral reef formation require relatively shallow water?

options.text: Enough light must reach the reef

questions.explanation: Shallow depth allows sufficient light to reach the reef; depth is one of the factors for coral reef formation.

questions.stem: Abiotic variables can affect the distribution of:
questions.explanation: Abiotic variables affect the distribution of both plants and animals, each within their range of tolerance.

questions.stem: Studying named species' adaptations to deserts and rainforests illustrates that:

options.text: Adaptations match organisms to their habitats

questions.explanation: Examples of adaptations in named desert and rainforest species show how adaptations match organisms to their habitats.

questions.stem: The habitat of a fish species could be described as:

options.text: A particular coral reef in a tropical sea

questions.explanation: A habitat is a place, such as a specific coral reef, including geographical and physical location and ecosystem type.

questions.stem: The statement that 'for any given temperature and rainfall one natural ecosystem is likely to develop' means abiotic factors:

options.text: Strongly determine the type of biome

questions.explanation: Temperature and rainfall largely determine which terrestrial biome develops in a region.

questions.stem: Which list contains only terrestrial biomes?

options.text: Tropical forest, taiga, grassland, tundra, hot desert
Coral reef, open ocean, pond, river

questions.explanation: Recognised terrestrial biomes include tropical forest, temperate forest, taiga, grassland, tundra and hot desert.

questions.scenario: A dune grass has deep, spreading roots and narrow, rolled leaves.
questions.stem: These features most likely help it to:

options.text: Anchor in shifting sand and reduce water loss

questions.explanation: Such features are adaptations to the abiotic conditions of dry, shifting sand dunes.

questions.stem: If sea temperature rises too far above the tolerance of corals, reefs may:

options.text: Be damaged, as temperature is a key factor for reefs

questions.explanation: Temperature is one of the factors required for coral reef formation, so excessive warming can damage reefs.

questions.scenario: A species grows best at a particular light intensity and less well above or below it.
questions.stem: This describes its:

options.text: Range of tolerance for that abiotic variable

questions.explanation: A species performs best within its range of tolerance for an abiotic variable such as light intensity.

questions.stem: A transect is especially useful for studying how species change:

options.text: Across a gradient in an abiotic variable

questions.explanation: Transects reveal how species distribution changes along a gradient of an abiotic variable.

questions.stem: Convergent evolution contributes to biome similarity by producing:

options.text: Similar features in unrelated species facing similar conditions

questions.explanation: Convergent evolution produces similar adaptations in unrelated species under similar abiotic conditions, giving biomes their similarity.

questions.stem: The habitat of a community differs from a niche because a habitat is:

options.text: A place, whereas a niche is a role
A role, whereas a niche is a place
The same thing as a niche

questions.explanation: Habitat is where organisms live (a place); a niche is the role a species plays in its ecosystem.

questions.scenario: A region has high temperature and high rainfall all year.
questions.stem: Which biome is most likely?
questions.explanation: Consistently high temperature and rainfall favour the development of tropical forest.

questions.stem: Adaptations of rainforest plants often address the challenge of:

options.text: Competing for light in dense vegetation

questions.explanation: In tropical rainforests, competition for light is a key challenge, so many plant adaptations help capture light.

questions.stem: Coral reefs are used in this topic as an example of a:
questions.explanation: Coral reefs are used as an example of a marine ecosystem whose formation depends on several abiotic factors.

questions.stem: Which best explains why a species is found only in certain areas?

options.text: Its range of tolerance limits it to areas with suitable abiotic conditions

questions.explanation: A species is limited to areas where abiotic variables fall within its range of tolerance.
